title: Photography zine dummy book
subtitle: Making a hand-sewn dummy to plan a photo-book sequence
excerpt: A dummy book is a blank physical mock-up used to plan the sequence of images in a photobook or photography zine before committing to a final print. Making a hand-sewn A5 dummy from cartridge paper takes an hour and produces a booklet that can be filled with printed or drawn image thumbnails to test page turns, pacing, and spreads.
sourceNotes: Photography dummy book construction synthesised from photobook-making and hand-bookbinding practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: A blank or roughly sketched physical mock-up of a book or zine used to plan image sequence, layout, and pacing before final production. Standard practice in photography book-making and graphic novel development.
A simple sewing structure where the needle passes through all signatures at once through a single fold at the spine. The simplest sewn binding; appropriate for booklets up to about 40 pages.

content.text: lets you experience an image sequence as a physical object, turning pages and checking pacing, before any final output. The dummy does not need to be beautiful. It needs to stand in for the finished book.

For a 24-page A5 dummy: cut six A4 sheets of 90 gsm cartridge in half to give twelve A5 sheets. Fold each A5 sheet in half to produce a 4-page folded unit. Stack all six folded units together to form a single signature. The dummy has a cover plus 22 interior pages.

Open the folded stack and pierce three sewing stations along the spine fold with the bookbinding awl: one at the centre fold, and two at 15 mm from each end. Pierce through all layers at once while the booklet is held open in a piercing jig or over a rounded edge so the awl has clearance on the far side.

structure. Thread the needle with 300 mm of linen thread (wax the thread if it is not pre-waxed). Enter the centre hole from the outside. Pass the thread inside the booklet to the head hole, exit outside, run the thread across the outside back to the tail hole, enter inside, and return inside to the centre hole, exiting outside. Tie off with a reef knot over the long thread that spans the spine.

Number each page lightly in pencil in the gutter (the margin nearest the spine) so the order is clear when rearranging. Tape or glue small prints, drawings, or photocopies in position. Use sticky notes for images you are not sure about. Work through the book turning pages as a reader would: the physical act of page-turning reveals pacing decisions that a flat plan does not.
